# LegalSeal Protocol

> Cryptographic attorney attestation protocol for AI-generated legal documents.
> Lawyers use LegalSeal to create verifiable, tamper-proof proof that they
> reviewed AI-generated legal output before it leaves the firm.

## What LegalSeal Does

LegalSeal provides the technical infrastructure for attorneys to cryptographically
sign attestations proving they reviewed AI-generated legal documents. It uses
SHA-256 hashing to create tamper-proof verification records without ever storing
the original document content. Over 716 lawyers have been sanctioned for filing
unverified AI output — LegalSeal is the open-source standard for preventing this.

## How It Works

1. The AI-generated document is hashed with SHA-256 (document never stored)
2. The reviewing attorney enters their name, bar number, and jurisdiction
3. The attorney signs an attestation statement confirming review
4. A verification receipt is generated with the hash, credentials, and timestamp
5. The receipt can be filed with courts or retained for compliance records

## Key Resources

- [Homepage & Tool](https://legalseal.io): Main site and document sealing tool
- [Protocol Specification](https://github.com/KevGrandWizard247/legalseal-protocol-/blob/main/protocol/SPEC.md): Full technical specification v1.0
- [GitHub Repository](https://github.com/KevGrandWizard247/legalseal-protocol-): Open source codebase (MIT license)
- [API Documentation](https://github.com/KevGrandWizard247/legalseal-protocol-/blob/main/README.md): REST API endpoints and setup guide

## Who It's For

- Solo practitioners using AI drafting tools (ChatGPT, Harvey, CoCounsel)
- Law firms implementing AI compliance workflows
- Legal departments documenting AI-assisted work product
- Courts requiring proof of human review for AI-generated filings

## Why It Matters

Courts across the US now require attorneys to verify AI-generated work. The ABA's
Formal Opinion 512 (2024) confirmed lawyers' ethical obligation to review AI output.
Notable cases include Mata v. Avianca (fabricated citations), Park v. Kim ($5,000
sanctions), and hundreds more. LegalSeal provides the missing technical standard
for proving that review happened.
